summary:
In individuals recovering from poliomyelitis who exhibit hand-to-knee gait, weakness primarily affects the quadriceps muscles; however, it may also involve additional lower limb muscles like glutes, hams, abductors, etc., contributing to altered movement patterns as part of an adaptive response during rehabilitation processes aimed towards functional improvement despite residual deficits caused by neurological damage sustained earlier infection events. Agent
 To address your requirements accurately:

Hand-to-knee gait observed in patients with polio results mainly from weakened quadriceps causing difficulty extending hips properly when standing up normally. However, involvement extends beyond just these muscles including others such as gluts, hams, and abductors, reflecting broader changes needed for ambulation adaptation after nerve injury impacts leg strength significantly.
